coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photoresponsive polyethyleneimine/citric acid co-carbonized dots for facile and selective sensing and intracellular imaging of cobalt ions at physiologic pH
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Wen-Sheng
Zhao, Qing-Chun
Zhang, Jun
Chen, Xiao-Ming
Wang, Xiu-Fang
Zhao, Lin
Chen, Shao-Hua
Wang, Ya-Qin
coredata.dc:description: Whether as an important biological element or as a radioactive source/medicine, the monitoring of trace levels of cobalt ions (Co) has become a non-negligible factor for human health and green environment. Current technologies for the detection of Co are cost-expensive and time-consuming, and require cumbersome sample pretreatment process. Herein a novel sensing platform has been developed for Co detection based on the quenching of the enhanced fluorescence signal of polyamine functionalized C-dots. Amine groups at the surface of the C-dots can capture Zn2+/Cd2+ to form coordination compound, which can inhibit the photoinduced electron transfer pathways of C-dots and then induce the fluorescence enhancement of the C-dots by ∼80% margin. Also, Co interacts with these amine groups to form an absorbent complex, which can strongly quench the enhanced fluorescence of C-dots via an inner filter effect. This C-dots-based probe showed a wide linear response to Co with a concentration ranging from 0.012 to 12 μM, and a detection limit of 8.0 nM and RSD of 5.7% (n = 5). Significantly, the C-Dots exhibit excellent properties, such as negligible cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, low-cost and high photostability, etc., which make C-dots favorable for label-free monitoring of Co and then successfully applied to the confocal imaging of intracellular Co.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent C-dots
Intracellular imaging
Photoinduced electron transfer (PET)
Inner filter effect
Cobalt detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent response of enhanced C-dots toward co
Selectivity of enhanced C-dots for Co
Possible response mechanism of C-dots toward Zn2+/Cd2+ and Co
Applications of C-dots in real sample assay and intracellular imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XPS (A) and FTIR spectra of C-dots (C). High-resolution XPS data of C1s (B).

Normalized UV-vis, excitation and emission spectra of C-dots (A). pH-dependent emission of C-dots (B). Insets in A-B are the photograph taken under 365 nm UV lamp and plot of intensity vs pH, respectively.

Spectra of Zn2+ titrating pure C-dots solution (A) and Co titrating C-dots solution enhanced by 10 μM Zn2+ (B). Spectra of Cd2+ titrating pure C-dots solution (C) and Co titrating C-dots solution enhanced by 10 μM Cd2+ (D). Insets in A and C are the plot of intensity vs concentration of Zn2+ and Cd2+, respectively. The red curves in Insets in A and C are the fitted data trends ones, respectively. Insets in B are photographs of pure C-dots (1), C-dots enhanced by 10 μM Zn2+ (2) and 10 μM Co quenched enhanced C-dots (3), respectively. Concentrations of C-dots are all ∼16 μg mL-1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Selectivity of the C-dots sensing platform for Co over other ions in pH ∼7.4. The concentrations of metal ions were 10 μΜ (A). The effect of potential biological interfering substances on detection of Co in cell (B). The concentrations of Co, calf thymus DNA, BSA, glutathione, various amino acids and glucose were 10, 0.1, 0.1, 0.1, 1.0 and 1000 μΜ, respectively.
Fluorescence decay of C-dots (A), and Zn2+ (B) and Co (C) titrating C-dots in turn in aqueous solution.
Schematic illustration of C-dots (A) and that for the responses of C-dots to Zn2+/Cd2+ (B) and Co (C), respectively.
Co concentration-dependent spectra of C-dot at pH 7.4. Inset: plot of log(F0/F) of C-dots vs concentration of Co.

Cellular imaging of C-dots. (A–E) are washed Cells imaged under confocal fluorescence of C-dots before (A) and after the addition of Zn2+ (B), and after the continual addition of Co (C), bright field (D), overlap of corresponding bright field image and fluorescence image (E), respectively.
One-step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy for preparation of polyamine functionalized C-Dots.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photoresponsive polyethyleneimine/citric acid co-carbonized dots for facile and selective sensing and intracellular imaging of cobalt ions at physiologic pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Building Materials and Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, Anhui Jianzhu University, 856 South Jinzhai Road, Hefei, Anhui 230022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Anhui Key Laboratory of Advanced Building Materials and Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Whether as an important biological element or as a radioactive source/medicine, the monitoring of trace levels of cobalt ions (Co) has become a non-negligible factor for human health and green environment. Current technologies for the detection of Co are cost-expensive and time-consuming, and require cumbersome sample pretreatment process. Herein a novel sensing platform has been developed for Co detection based on the quenching of the enhanced fluorescence signal of polyamine functionalized C-dots. Amine groups at the surface of the C-dots can capture Zn2+/Cd2+ to form coordination compound, which can inhibit the photoinduced electron transfer pathways of C-dots and then induce the fluorescence enhancement of the C-dots by ∼80% margin. Also, Co interacts with these amine groups to form an absorbent complex, which can strongly quench the enhanced fluorescence of C-dots via an inner filter effect. This C-dots-based probe showed a wide linear response to Co with a concentration ranging from 0.012 to 12 μM, and a detection limit of 8.0 nM and RSD of 5.7% (n = 5). Significantly, the C-Dots exhibit excellent properties, such as negligible cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, low-cost and high photostability, etc., which make C-dots favorable for label-free monitoring of Co and then successfully applied to the confocal imaging of intracellular Co.

ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt (Co) as an important biological element plays significant roles in the metabolism of iron and synthesis of hemoglobin, which acts as a stimulant for the production of red blood cells [1–4]. Co deficiency and overload can both disturb the cellular homeostasis and induce various biological disorders. It is found in vitamin B12 in minute amounts, which is essential for humans, and the recommended daily intake of vitamin B12 is about 6 μg. However, the excessive Co uptake can lead to vasodilatation, flushing and cardiomyopathy in humans and animals, etc. [5,6]. The oral LD50 (lethal dose, 50%) value of soluble Co salts in rats is about 150–500 mg kg−1 [7]. Also, Co is a major cause of contact dermatitis and is considered as a possible carcinogenic for humans [8–10]. Therefore, the residual level of Co in drinking water is restricted by certain governments' health policies. The maximum level of Co in drinking water permitted should be lower than 40 μg L-1 [11]. The radioactive cobalt species (60Co) is another non-negligible factor for menacing human health. 60Co is a very strong radioactive species that can result in alopecia, and seriously damage to human blood cells within the organization, which makes leukocyte less and gives rise to hematological diseases such as aplastic anemia, leukemia, and even death [12]. A Japanese teleplay titled Akai Giwaku was ever swept over East Asia in 1970s, which told about the innocent and kind Oshima Yukiko suffered from leukemia due to an abrupt biochemical radiation of 60Co at her father's laboratory. In synopsis, Oshima Yukiko only lived for one and a half years after illness, and a flower of life faded via a short-lived blooming. 60Co is usually used in the radiotherapy of cancer and tumor, and so on. As a result, radioactive wastewater is discharged by a large number of medical research institutions [13]. The maximum levels of 60Co in air of radioactive workplace and in open water permitted are 0.33 and 370 BqL−1, respectively. The miserable tale on 60Co and harms to health of Co indicate that detections of Co species are of great importance and urgency in the chemical, biomedical and environmental fields.

In recent years, it has attracted considerable research efforts to develop facile and rapid approaches for monitoring of trace levels of cobalt due to the gradually increasing needs for human health and green environment. Several elegant techniques have been reported for detection of Co, such as inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry [14], flame atomic absorption spectrometry [15,16], electrochemical approaches [17], UV-vis absorbance [18–22], surface-enhanced Raman scattering and colorimetric assay based on modified Au/Ag nanoparticles etc. [18,19,23]. However, Current technologies for the detection of Co are cost-expensive and time-consuming, and require cumbersome sample pretreatment process, which makes the on-the-spot and real-time monitoring of Co ion impossible. Recently, because subtle changes of the surface properties of photoluminescent materials can result in dramatic changes in their optical properties, several efficient fluorescent sensors for detection of Co were reported [24–28]. Compared with other techniques, fluorescence probes provide several advantages such as high specificity and sensitivity, localized information at the target site, even real-time monitoring in living cells, tissues, and animals [29]. Unfortunately, no attention has, so far, been paid on exploiting highly selective and sensitive probes for real-time and in vivo monitoring of Co in living systems in order to further understand the role that Co plays in biological and pathological events. Therefore, it is still a continuous demand to develop cheap, easy to operate, environmentally friendly, and reliable intracellular fluorescence probes for this purpose.

Herein, a one-step “synthesis-modification integration” strategy is carried out for synthesis of highly bright carbon dots (C-dots) using citric acid (CA)/polyethyleneimine (PEI) as precursors [30]. The as-prepared C-dots can realize direct functionalization by PEI in synthesis process, and no additional surface modifications for introducing binding sites are required. The pH-dependent emission of C-dots was observed, and optimal pH centered at 4.2. Most interestingly, the fluorescence intensity can quickly recover to maximum with addition of Cd2+/Zn2+ at physiologic pH (∼7.4), which, after that, can be completely quenched by Co under masking of Cu2+ by thiourea and Fe3+ by sodium hexametaphoshpate (SHPP). Accordingly, a facile and effective fluorescent platform with polyamine modified C-dots as the signal output for detection of Co was constructed, resulting in a linear fluorescence quenching in range of 0.012–12 μM with 8 nM detection limit. Importantly, the C-dots were also applied in intracellular imaging of Co, exhibiting the excellent photostability, low toxicity, good biocompatibility and high water solubility of C-dots. Due to simplicity, selectivity and availability, the C-dots have great promise as a practical platform for sensing and intracellular imaging of Co.

PEI (purity over 99%, MW = 10 000) and SHPP (purity 99%) were the products of Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China). All other reagents were of analytical grade and commercially purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), and used without further purification. Dialysis bag (molecular weight cutoff = 10 000) was acquired from Shanghai Green Bird Science & Technology Development Co., China).

The fluorescence spectra and UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded with an F-4600 (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan) and a Hitachi UV-2910 UV-vis spectrophotometer, respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured from a KBr window on a Nicolet-6700 spectrophotometer (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the product were carried out on a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL, JSM 7500F). Characterizations of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were taken on a JEM-200CX (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) microscope operating at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed with an XPS instrument (ThermoESCALAB250, USA) using Al Ka X-ray source (1486.6eV). The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected on a Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The acidity was measured with a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (Sartorius, Dietikon, Switzerland). The fluorescence lifetime measurements were recorded on an Edinburgh Analytical Instruments F900 using hydrogen lamp as a light source and 365 nm as an excitation wavelength. Decay curve for C-dots was monitored at 450 nm. The solution containing C-dots was analyzed for ξ-potential values using dynamic light scattering (Nano-Z, Malvern, UK) with laser wavelength at 633 nm and a measurement angle of 173° (backscatter detection) at 25 °C.

The preparation of C-dots was mainly referred to a previous report with slight modification [31]. Briefly, 0.2 g of CA and 0.36 g of PEI were added into 15 mL water. The mixed solution was ultrasonic for 30 min, and then transferred to the Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the as-prepared C-dots were centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 10 min to remove large precipitates. For further purifying the C-dots, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h. The purified C-dots were preserved in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use.

The cytotoxicity study of as-prepared C-dots was performed using the MTT assay on HeLa cells by following standard protocols. HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (DMEM) medium with 10% fetal bovine serum and 100 μg/mL penicillin/streptomycin and maintained at 37 °C, 95% relative humidity, and 5% CO2. After incubating for 24 h, the medium was replaced with 100 μL of fresh medium containing a concentration of C-dots (from 0, 25, 50, 100 to 200 μgmL−1). At time interval of 24 h or 48 h, the medium was removed before addition of fresh medium (100 μL) containing MTT (20 μL, 5 mgmL−1) into each well. After incubating the cells for another 2 h, the absorbance of the solution was measured to assess the relative viabilities of the cells by a Bio-Rad 680 microplate reader. So did the cytotoxicity study of as-prepared C-dots with Zn2+. Optical density (OD) was read at a wavelength of 490 nm. The cell viability was estimated according to the following equation (1).
(1)
Cell
viability
=
OD
Treated
/OD
Control
× 100 %
where ODControl and ODTreated are the optical density in the absence and in the presence of C-dots, respectively.

HeLa cells (1 × 104) were plated in a cover-glass–bottom dish in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. After incubating for 24 h, the cells were incubated with ∼50 μgmL−1 C-dots for 4 h. Prior to fixation of the cells on the slide for inspection with a fluorescence microscope, the excess C-dots were removed by washing with warm phosphate buffered salt for three times. After that, HeLa cells were treated with the same medium supplemented with 50 μM ZnCl2 for 0.5 h, cell imaging for Zn2+ enhancement was performed after repeatedly washing the cells with PBS to remove excess C-dots. Then culture medium and 50 μM CoCl2 were added into the dish. The images were immediately taken by a confocal laser scanning microscope.

The water samples were collected in a precleaned glass bottle. The samples were filtered through 0.45 μm Supor filters or centrifuged to remove large particles after the pH was modulated to 7.4 or so, and then stored in a refrigerator. Because no Co in the collected water samples was detectable by ICPMS method, recovery tests were carried out on the samples spiked with 0.1, 1.0, and 5.0 μM Co to evaluate their reliabilities.

In recent decade, C-dots have attracted tremendous attention due to their remarkable characteristics in high resistance to photobleaching, high water dispersibility, robust chemical inertness, low cost and toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [32–36]. As a fascinating class of newly synthesized nanomaterials, C-dots have been widely used in bioimaging, drug delivery, fluorescence sensing, optoelectronic devices, etc. [37–39]. Interestingly, the precursors of the C-dots are not limited in highly pure chemical reagents, and the raw materials for C-dots are nearly hands-down [40]. What's more, the synthesis condition for C-dots is relatively simple. In comparison to that for traditional fluorescence dyes, the troublesome and time-consuming separation and purification processes are not the requisite, and the highly pure products are obtained only by simple centrifuging or dialyzing due to nanometer-sized effect. In view of this, we attempt to synthesize highly bright C-dots by hydrothermal pathway for sensing and intracellular imaging Co.

To explore the photophysical properties of the C-dots, the UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence were studied at room temperature. The UV–vis absorption and normalized optimal excitation and emission spectra of C-dots shown in Fig. 2 A reveal the peak absorption, maximum excitation and emission wavelengths centered at 242 nm with a broad shoulder absorption ranging from 270 to 425 nm, 362 nm and 448 nm, respectively. Under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp, the C-dots can emit brightly blue light (see inset in Fig. 2A). The QY of C-dots in water was calculated as ∼0.45 by the slope method using the reference of quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 0.54) [44], suggesting the tremendous potential of our C-dots for bioimaging. The photoluminescence peak at 448 nm can be excited in a narrow wavelength range. The main photoluminescence excitation band well agrees with the corresponding defect-state absorption bands, suggesting that the emission is characterized by defect-state decay rather than bandedge exciton-state decay [45].

The excitation-dependent emission is a common phenomenon for C-dots [46,47], which allows for multiple colors emit via photoluminescence under different excitation wavelengths, and is of great significance for two-photonisc imaging and biomedical applications due to the blue excitation [48]. As excitation wavelengths increased from 310 to 420 nm with an interval of 10 nm, the corresponding emission wavelengths gradually red-shifted from 430 to 490 nm. Meanwhile, the emissions firstly enhanced, and then gradually decreased with the feature of 360 nm as a maximum excitation wavelength (see Fig. S4). Also, pH-dependent emission behavior is one of the most special properties of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 2B, with pH being increased from 1 to 12, the emission enhanced firstly to max at pH ∼4.2, then gradually decreased (see inset in Fig. 2B), which possibly caused by the presence of many –NH2 and a little COO- group in the C-dots [40]. These groups are ampholytes and possess varying isoelectric points and dissociation constants. Moreover, when temperature was leaped repeatedly at 20 and 60 °C, the F/F0 values kept unchanged (see Fig. S5), exhibiting an excellent stability against temperature, as well as a high resistant to photobleaching and an independence of ionic strength of medium (data not shown).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of enhanced C-dots toward co
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned above, the maximal emission of C-dots was observed at pH 4.2, and about half decay in intensity at physiologic pH (∼7.4) (see Fig. 2B and inset). In order to better understand the potential of C-dots in the fields of intracellular imaging and sensing, all experiments were performed in aqueous solution buffered with Tris-HCl at pH 7.4, and effects of Co ions on fluorescence spectra of C-dots were investigated. The result indicates that fluorescence gradually reduced by 72% with Co being injected into ∼32 μg mL-1 C-dots aqueous solution to 30 μM (see Fig. S6), implying there should be a useful relationship between Co concentration and quenched fluorescence that can be adopted for quantitative analysis of Co. Meanwhile, a most interesting phenomenon that gradual injection of Zn2+ or Cd2+ into C-dots aqueous solution to 10 μM results in fluorescence enhancement by ∼80% margin was observed (see Fig. 3 A). In general, in the presence of a given quencher concentration, the lower the concentration of fluorophore, the larger the ratio change of original and instant intensity, and the higher the sensitivity was found [49]. In that way, given the probe enhanced by Zn2+ or Cd2+ as a sensing platform for Co, compared with the pure C-dots aqueous solution as a probe, the former would possibly hold higher sensitivity and signal-to-noise ratio (S/N), which should be valuable in Co detection. The response rate of the C-dots enhanced by Zn2+ toward Co was then investigated. The result shown in Fig. 3B indicates the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was quenched over 70% when Co concentration injected into the C-dots solution was near to 10 μM, verifying the above inference and revealing that fluorescence intensity of C-dots is sensitive to Co concentration. The probe enhanced by Cd2+ gave the same result (see Fig. 3C–D). Due to much stronger cytotoxicity of Cd2+ ions, the protocol enhanced by Zn2+ ions was adopted in next experiments, and the probe was designed as 16 μg L−1 of C-dots and 10 μM of Zn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of enhanced C-dots for Co
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity is another important parameter to evaluate the performance of the sensing platform. To evaluate the selectivity of the present probe for Co, the fluorescence intensity changes in the presence of representative metal ions were examined under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 4 A, besides Co, Zn2+ and Cd2+, the effects of other 12 kinds of cations, including Ni2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Li+, Mn2+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, NH4 +, and Cu2+, on the fluorescent responses of C-dots were investigated. Most other cations have no effects on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. As shown in Fig. 3A and C, 10 μM of Zn2+ and Cd2+ can maximally enhance the fluorescence intensity of C-dots with given concentration (16 μg L−1). The concentrations of Zn2+ and Cd2+ increased again result in the unchanged intensity was observed. That is the exotic Zn2+ and Cd2+ with concentration ranging from 0 to 20 μM have no obvious effects on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. However, it can be seen from Fig. 4A that the presence of Cu2+ leads to a dramatic decrease of C-dots in intensity, indicating that this metal ion at high concentrations seriously interferes with Co detection. Meanwhile, Fe3+ exhibited a relatively slight interference to the detection of Co. Most fortunately, these issues can be effectively avoided by using SHPP [50] and thiourea as masking agents for Fe3+ and Cu2+, respectively. Indeed, the additions of Fe3+ and Cu2+ into the C-dots aqueous solution in the presence of SHPP and thiourea gave no effect on the fluorescence intensity (see Fig. S7A). Similarly, the additions of Fe3+ and Cu2+ into the aqueous solution mixed by C-dots and Co in the presence of SHPP and thiourea gave no effect on the detection of Co (see Fig. S7B). Also, the effects of biomolecules such as amino acid, DNA, protein and sugar as representatives on the detection of Co in cell were investigated to understand the application potential of probe. The results shown in Fig. 4B indicate little amount of the above biomolecules have no interference for Co detection. All these observations indicate that the present probe exhibits high selectivity for Co. The facile, effective and selective probe for Co is therefore established utilizing PEI/CA co-carbonized dots.

Known to all, for photoluminescent nanomaterials, subtle changes of the surface properties generated by synthesis condition, chemicals difference etc., can result in dramatic changes in their optical properties [51]. Our present probe and other reported C-dots platform using different PEI and CA as precursors exhibited diverse responses to different stimuli seems to be unsurprising [41,43,52,53], which is possibly one of the charms of chemistry. Factually, this probe can be developed as a sensitive sensing platform for Cu2+ under the simultaneous masking of Co and Fe3+. Two causes include: 1) a large number of photoluminescence sensors for Cu2+ have been reported; 2) lack of special masking agent for Co, both of which result in the endeavors aiming at developing Cu2+ sensor were given up.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of C-dots toward Zn2+/Cd2+ and Co
ce:section.ce:para: Photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process as an important class of photochromic phenomenon is an attractive investigated hotspots in constructions of molecule-level devices because of their unique molecular recognition ability and signal transduction function [54,55]. PET molecules emit no or weak fluorescence due to the existence of PET pathways from receptors containing lone pair electron groups such as amine nitrogen, azamacrocycles, crown ester, nitrogen and oxygen heterocycles, etc., to fluorophore across spacer. Upon protonation [56,57] or coordination with metal ions [54,55,58,59], a dramatic increase of the fluorescence intensity results as PET quenching is deactivated. Our C-dots are the polyamine functionalized nanomaterials, which holds the characteristic of above PET activation (see Fig. 5 A). As shown in Figs. 2B and 3A and C, both protonation from physiologic pH (∼7.4) to pH ∼4.2 and coordination with Zn2+/Cd2+ of C-dots can deactivate this quenching relay. Furthermore, no effect of EDTA molecule on the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was observed (see Fig. S8A), the addition of EDTA into the C-dots aqueous solution maximally enhanced by Zn2+ decreased the fluorescence intensity to original level (see Fig. S8B). The lifetime measurements of Zn2+ and Co2+ titrating C-dots in turn were also carried out to understand the interactions between metal ions and C-dots. The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves shown in Fig. 5 were measured by the time-correlated single photon counting method. The decay curves fit all well to a tri-exponent nature as demonstrated by the residuals. The fluorescence mean lifetimes of C-dots, and Zn2+ and Co titrating C-dots are calculated to be 5.43, 5.56 and 5.48 ns, respectively, which are a typical fluorescence decay level and indicate the coordination between metal ions and amines on the surface of C-dots. Therefore, the explanation on fluorescence enhancement mechanism was obtained (see Fig. 6 A–B).

The absorption spectrum of absorbent possesses a complementary overlap region with the excitation or emission spectra of fluorophore, the fluorescence emission of fluorophore can be quenched, which is the inner filter effect and an alternative approach to convert absorbance signal into fluorescence one for stimuli assay [60,61]. The UV−vis absorptions of Co interacting with C-dots and PEI in aqueous solution were investigated. The results exhibit that C-dots have two absorption bands centered at 243 and 348 nm (see Fig. S10A). However, two new UV-vis absorption bands were found when the addition of Co into the C-dots aqueous solution. One absorption band centered at 285 nm, the other broad shoulder one located at range from 450 to 650 nm (see Fig. S9A), which is in accordance with the absorption of Co titrating PEI aqueous solution (see Fig. S9B). The two new absorption bands can be attributed to the cobaltous amine that resulted from the coordination of Co with the amine groups at the surface of C-dots, which was verified by the fact that when the much stronger chelating reagent EDTA was added the C-dots-Co system, the fluorescence intensity can be most recovered (see Fig. S10). The solution was then dialyzed for 24 h, the new absorption peaks generated by cobaltous amine complex disappeared (data not shown). The absorption band centered at 285 nm and the broad shoulder absorption band ranged at 450–650 nm partially overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of the C-dot, respectively. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the C-dots by Co may result from the inner filter effect related to the specific UV-vis absorption of cobaltous amine (see Fig. 6C). Analogue report has been published by Dong et al. [43] Specially pointed out, most metal ions can coordinate with PEI, for example, Zn2+ and Cd2+ in the present work, but the formed complexes have no inner filter effect on the C-dots and thus have nearly no interference in the detection of Co.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of C-dots in real sample assay and intracellular imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots display a high fluorescence brightness, and the amine groups at the surface of C-dots have a high affinity to Co. Thus it can be expected the C-dots probe to detect the trace Co analyte in aqueous solution. As shown in Fig. 7 , the C-dots have a distinct linearly quenching toward Co in the concentration range of 0.012–12 μM with a correlation coefficient over 0.99 and a linear regression equation of Y = 0.0423C + 0.04207 (where C is the concentration of Co in μM, Y is the logarithmic value of quenching efficiency (F0/F)). The detection limit of Co was calculated to be 8 nM (S/N = 3). The precision for five replicate detections of 5.0 μM Co was 5.7% (RSD). The quantitative recoveries located at range from were 95.8%–107.4% for the Yihai lake water, sewage water, waste liquor from paper plant and waste liquor from corrosion of Cu spiked with 0.1, 1.0 and 5.0 μM Co, respectively. The applicability of this sensing platform for detecting Co in real samples was further evaluated. The above-mentioned water samples spiked by 0.5 and 2.0 μM Co, respectively, were analyzed for Co. The concentrations of Co in the spiked river samples determined by the proposed method were in good agreement with those of added Co, demonstrating the potential applicability of C-dots for the detection of Co (see Table S1). Also, these values are consistent with those obtained by the ICPMS method.

Cell cytotoxicity experiments of C-dots were evaluated using Hela cells through the MTT viability assay. The obtained C-dots show no apparent toxicity to the cells even though the C-dots concentration was increased to 100 mg L-1 and incubated for 48 h (see Fig. S11A). The C-dots with Zn2+ gives the same result (see Fig. S11B). The C-dots exhibit bright photoluminescent properties, low toxicity, and high photostability, which make them promising probes for bioimaging applications. Hence, experiments were carried out to further demonstrate the availability of the as-prepared C-Dots enhanced by Zn2+ for imaging intracellular Co in Hela cells. HeLa cells were cultured in a culture medium, and confocal microscopic images were taken using a 405-nm laser at a low voltage. As shown in Fig. 8 , after incubation of C-dots with HeLa cells, the blue emissions of C-dots sample could be clearly observed from intracellular region of HeLa cells when excited at 405 nm (see Fig. 8A). The emissions mainly located at the areas of the cell membrane and cytoplasm, especially around the cell nucleus. Whereas the brightness of C-dots was very weak inside the cell nucleus [46,47,62]. To further investigate if our probe could be applied for semi-quantitative monitoring of the intracellular Co level, exogenous Zn2+ and Co was introduced in turn into the HeLa cells pretreated by C-dots. Upon supplementing cells with 50 μM of Zn2+ and Co in the growth medium for 0.5 h at 37 °C, respectively, as expected, microscope images showed very strong intracellular fluorescence for the former (see Fig. 8B) and very weak or no intracellular fluorescence for the latter (see Fig. 8C), indicating that our probe could be applied for semi-quantitative imaging intracellular Co [63]. Importantly, the cytotoxicity of C-dots was negligible as confirmed by the facts that no significant damage in cell morphology (see Fig. 8D) and cell viability (see Fig. S11) were observed after the treatments of Hela cells with C-dots, indicating the C-dots have great potentials in intracellular imaging and other biomedical applications.

In summary, PEI and CA were co-carbonized at 200 °C for 5 h to prepare polyamine functionalized C-dots with high QY and high photostability. Amine groups at the surface of the C-dots can complex with Zn2+/Cd2+ to inhibit the PET pathways, resulting in the fluorescence enhancement of the C-dots by ∼80% margin. Also, Co interacts with these amine groups to form an absorbent complex, which can sensitively quench the enhanced fluorescence of C-dots via an inner filter effect. An enhanced photoresponsive probe was therefore developed for facile and selective sensing of Co in environmental samples at physiologic pH. More notably, the C-dots-based sensing platform shows many advantages, including negligible cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, high sensitivity, wide linear response range, and low cost, being promising as an efficient platform for quantitatively monitoring the intracellular Co.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of China (21506002, 21671003), and Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1608085MB37, 1408085MB27) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2017.03.018.
